words: They were just wound up after the There used to be one here, just north. He was east of the Division Street Bridge. He used to be a jungle camp. All the boys they grew up there and jungle up there. They lived there until we got a job and went out and searched in the winter. They used to be here. They closed down about the 1st of December. They'd all come into Spokane until about the 1st of April. Then you'd start sending them back out in the woods, the camps. They wanted to open up and work all summer. Then here they'd go broke and they'd go out in the jungle camp and jungle up. Down in Peaceful Valley, L .F. is all down there. We're all little shacks and so forth. Everybody had a shack and they lived in a shack down there. Two or three of them together in a place to lay out a fight. Then one of them they'd move out. That was no other way for them to live because they'd be involved. That's one involved. That was only a small thing. Sometimes we know nobody there. Sometimes one or two. I don't think there's any more than half a dozen there at any time. That's right. There used to be 100 of them. There used to be maybe 150, 200 down in Peaceful Valley down there. Also back out in this jungle over here with this post office. It's a terminal for them. The post office is a terminal. The fellas on the railroad stopped off there too. They'd stay for two or three days and then they'd move on.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That was their life.

words: You say jungle up.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: They carried your bed in those days. You had your bundle on your back so you just laid under a tree anyway.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Something like the jungle camp. They have one down here now down west of the Monroe Street Bridge here about a half mile. It's about 100 down there shacking up right now. Gee, I didn't know that. Some have got tents up. Some just sleep on the ground. They don't leave a dam where they're at. They have a little place there, a little kettle, a frying pan. They cook their meals and so forth. They all go in together kind of common.

words: Communion.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: No lumberjack. And these fellas down here now, they steal anything to get their hands out of. They don't give a damn to get full of dough and so forth. With the old lumberjacks, you get a bottle of whiskey and you drink it up and get drunk, but they'd wake up in the morning and they'd be sober.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: But these fellas there on the tail all over. They'd never come down. If any old guy is a shirt off your back and you'd get it back or they'd pay you, there was never any dead breach that I would get. If one of you had given him a dollar, he'd never forget it until he paid it back.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That was one thing about him. First I was here in Spokane, we're in the love of Frank Featherston, Featherston's employment agency, and we hired for the Potlatch. That's right. I heard him down there. We sent thousands of men out there. Well, well, from the time they started up, they closed down. And then of course we had the fellas all lined up for when they'd go back out in the spring, and they'd all be waiting for their job. All be broke.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they'd come in to you and you'd fix them up with the job?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. I'd give them a ticket for the job up there, and we'd charge them for the job, but they never had their money, so we'd trust them for it. And they'd go up money and they'd always send it back. You never had to worry about it. Of course we charged $2 in those days for a job. Now you have to pay 10 % of your first month's salary or something like that. Yeah, that's changed a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you pay their way out to the job, too?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Lots of them. We knew them and they were all right, you know. Of course the company would call in for certain fellas, lots of times for certain jobs they had. Well, if they were broke, we'd pay their way out to the job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They asked you for a particular guy to be the man?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, oh yeah, sure. Fellas worked here for years, you know, and they'd come in, and then when they started up, they'd call in the superintendent along and they'd call in for certain guys and find them and send them out. And nine times out of ten, they'd be broke, and we'd give them the job and send them out. But 4th of July, come along, they'd all come back in and pay you. That's good. You could really trust them. You could trust them, then. Oh, yeah, you can never have any trouble. You could give them money and they'd pay it back. It was the last thing they ever did.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: About all those old fellas, you know, that's the first thing they done after they got their stake made before they get on the drunk and settle up their bills. You bet. After they had everything paid for and bought maybe a new shirt or a pair of shoes, then they'd poop away the rest. Yeah. I remember one night, I never forget, I fell up at the Diamond Match Club in Priest River.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He'd come in town and got drunk, and there was a couple guys after him, see, and I happened to be going by the old National Bank about 9 o 'clock at night. Of course, our office was only a block and a half down the street from there. We were on main at that time. We were down on, you see, Stevens and Trent. And he handed me his pocketbook. He says, take care of this for me, Joe. And I says, okay. And I just said, and he says, don't stop me. Just keep it going. I don't want these fellas to know I even know you. And he handed me his pocketbook, and I spun it in my pocket. So I come to the next corner, and I turned around, went back down towards the office, and I went down and put it in the same. And he was, he says, I'll see you tomorrow morning, 6 o 'clock. I said, okay. I got him next morning and come there, and he never showed up.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And I kept the pocketbook for about a week, and I thought, oh, what the hell happened to that guy?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He was from as much as I threw him in the river, and killed him. And so I took the pocketbook out, looked at it, and he had $35 ,000 in it. $35 ,000 in the pocketbook carrying around in his pocket. So I took it up to the bank and put it in the bank under his name, the old National Bank. And God, he never showed up. Two years went by. Never heard anything about him. And he had a home back in Minnesota. His father and mother were home people. They were living back there. And I had their address, so if he hadn't shut up while I was going to get in touch with him, we had the money transferred to him. But one morning in the first of April, two years after that one morning, in the first day of April, he walked in. I just opened up at 6 in the morning.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, hey, Joe, how are you?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, okay.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And I said, well, for Christ's sake, what have you been? He says, well, he says, you remember that night I saw you?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, yeah. He says, you know, it was two guys after me then. And he says, you know, they followed me. And I circled around down at the Great Northern. There was a freight train pulling by, and I jumped on the freight train and I landed over in Seattle. The next morning, I went up and got on a fishing boat and went to Alaska. I had been up there ever since.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, why the hell didn't you write me?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, hell. He said, nobody gives a goddamn about me. I didn't think anybody would be interested. And so I didn't say anything about the money because he didn't have nothing then.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, what are you going to do? He said, you got a job?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And I said, yeah. Well, he says, first he says, give me five dollars. I want to get breakfast and a pair of socks. And I said, okay. And I gave him five dollars and he went out and got his breakfast. And pretty soon he came back.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He says, you got a job for me?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, yeah. He says, oh, but going back in that job and the diamonds you had when you left, I said, the same camp and the same outfit there.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, Jesus, could you give me that?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: You know, he says, I used to make about two thousand a month up there. He had one of these bulldozers and operated his bulldozer. He did the gypos. And so I says, all right. And I set him up there. Well, I didn't hear anything from him till the fourth of July. And he come in. Of course, he come in that night. He got drunk in hell. So I didn't hear tell him then. So I waited about a week and finally sobered up and come in. He says, well, I staked him to go back on this job, you know, and give him pay spare up on a bus and give him enough money till he got to camp. And so he came in and paid me back the money. And he says, I'm going to keep one hundred dollars. He says, here's four thousand. He says, take care of it till I go back. I do want to carry it with me some son of a bitch to roll me like I did before.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, what did they roll you before?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He says, yeah, that's the reason I had to leave town. And he was sober then and ready to go back to work. I said, well, just let me give you a little surprise.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: You remember the night you passed me up in front of the old National Bank?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, he said, and those fellows were after me then.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, you remember you gave me your pocketbook?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Nah, I need to get you a pocketbook. He said, those bastards rolled me and took my pocketbook out all my money. I said, no, they didn't. I said, you gave me your pocketbook and you had thirty five thousand dollars. Oh, he says, you're crazy. He says, I didn't have nothing. He said, they took everything I had. Well, I said, let me show you.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: See this bank book?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: There's your name on it. And there was thirty five thousand dollars and there was two years interest on that up in the bank, waiting for you. He says, you're crazy. Well, I says, come on with me. You're up to the bank. So we were up to the bank and they said, yeah, and they told him the amount of interest he had in two years and thirty five thousand dollars. Well, he said, Jesus, don't let me take it out and keep it there. So he kept it and he went back to work. I put the other money that he gave me because he didn't want that either. So I took him put it up four thousand more with it. And of course, he bought it to him at forty thousand dollars. So he worked up the first of October. The first October he came in and he said, well, you know, he said, I'm going back home.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, what's the matter?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, my dad's sick and they don't expect him to have it. He says, you grew up in the have the help with me to get that money. I said, sure. So I went up and I said, now, wait a minute. Let's transfer this money back to the bank in your town. You don't want to carry that money with you. I will transfer it. The banker here will transfer it back to the bank in your town. You give us the name of the bank. So he gives the name of the bank. So we transferred. He had enough money with him without having to tell you that. So he transferred I think it's come to forty two thousand dollars back to this bank in the hometown where his parents were. And as I see from that date, it is. But I know the money get back there.
