words: Thirty five years ago.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That's some story. But that you have to remember that Dick Green. They just drive team. You know, he was an awful drunkard. And that he was a good guy when he was sober.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: The longer and beyond the drunk. How he said that five hundred dollars. And he was going back east and he had the ticket bought. And he had five hundred dollars plus the ticket. Of course, all his sons was laying for him to blow him in. And they finally got down some walkie deep over the bottle of booze and given to his new drinks. That was the end of that. Another drunk. And he spent the five hundred and never got out of town. Forget.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: See, well, Bob McGee worked for me and he used to come in and blow me and everything. So I said, Bob, I think you better stay in camp and not go to town. I said, save your money and also put it down the bank and Spirit Lake for you. Well, he put that we come to ten thousand dollars. And when he got ten thousand dollars, you got a letter from home and he was to go. They wanted him back home. His mother was very sick. Well, he got down and that's a full can. He got the ten thousand out of the bank and get downtown. And I saw him about a month afterwards. He never did leave town. He spent the whole ten thousand dollars and he was broke.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: So he says, I said, well, how about going back to camp?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he said, you wouldn't want me back there again. I sure do. I said, hey, job's waiting for you. He drove a four horse tote team, see. And I got I took him, paid his bill and took him back on the train to Spirit Lake with me and up in the lake, put him back on the job driving the four horse to you. And he worked, shared till he died. But those old fellas, you know, they had nothing to just come along and do everything. So they went away like water. All the girls downtown, the sporting girls and all of them, they made their living out of them. Well, I can remember when I was running camp with a fan there at Spirit Lake. They'd come up to tie, start up the first of April and the boys had come downtown and maybe took a hundred of them up on the train with me. Pat to pay their fare of all of them, get them out to camp. And then the first month, they'd been living with these girls up in the sporting houses all winter and they'd been carrying them along from the time they went broke, maybe for two or three months. And then the girls would come in the first two months and pick up their checks. They'd get them and sign them, pad them over to them.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That's the kind of people who were hard with me. Well, I've taken 200 men out of jail down here. Charlie Fleming was mayor there. I'd get out and try to make a deal with Charlie. I said, Charlie, you know, the city's going broke here and trying to feed all these guys. And I said, they're all fine guys. You got them in here for being drunk, that's all. I'd now take them all off your hands if you even go without any fine.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: By God, he said, would you do that?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, sure, well. I'd take them out of the train in the morning. I was hunting about 1 ,200 men up there then. They had four camps operating. Take them out on the train, pay their fare, take them back up, put them to work. So after three days, as soon as you get their liquor out of them, John, the best workers you ever see in your life. They'd never get their sires and be stripped right down of their waist, you know. Take their shirts off and hang them on a bush and work there all day. Work like hell. You never seen better workers, Dan, than they were, these old lumberjack.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: They got in camp. In those days, we'd had maybe 150, 200 men in the camp, see. And once in a while, some guy would steal something. Well, boy, those fellas would route him out. He was gone within our next day or two. You wouldn't have him around at all. You never had to say anything. Didn't make it so tough for him. He'd have to leave camp. So the consequence was they were honest and upright, good workers. You were better than anything you ever saw.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: They loved to work.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: But they loved to play, too, when they got in town. I just, it's so hard for me to understand how you could blow that much money that fast in those days when a dollar was worth a lot more than it is today. God, they must have lived high to be able to... Well, you know what the rates were when I started in 1915 for the Panhandle Ember Company of Spirit Lane. We worked 12 hours a day, and the men carried their own blankets on their back. And we paid 20 cents an hour to a swamper. We paid 24 cents an hour to a sawyer and a can of man, a bevy man. 20 cents and 24 cents an hour. Back in 1915, and those fellas would come up and work all summer long and come into town and go broken today.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, we had one fella.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He'd come into town. He'd stay in camp six months. He'd come into town, go up to some of these houses and pick up two or three girls, get a taxi cab, and he'd ride around and make every beer parlor in town, nightclub. And then the next day, he would take the taxi and go to Seattle. He'd make the clubs over there and the beer parlors and so forth. And then he'd come back, and he wouldn't have enough to pay his taxi bill, bring the girls back with him. He couldn't even pay the... I'd pay $200 a taxi bill. Yeah, just for him, you know, get back out of the camp. As soon as they pay, they come and send me in the money. Now, that... they just were that kind of guy. When they came in, they worked hard in the woods, and they played hard in town. And that was their life. You don't find them anymore. I used to know every man down in Trenton, Maine Avenue, by their first name. I'd go up to the street there, and most of them worked for me one time. That's after I had an employment office with Featherston. And most of them worked up... They'd come out there, you know, make a stake for a couple weeks, and then they had enough money to go someplace else. So they... way he'd go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? They'd come in to you for a stake to get out, and then they'd go someplace else?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: No, no. They'd come in for me for a stake to help us to go to the jobs. But they'd come in with a stake, but they'd be booked. And they'd go to the panhandle, that's only out in the fair, there's only $1 .40 up there. And they'd go out there, and then they'd work two or three weeks and get enough money to go some other place where they had a job where they could make more money.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I used to call them cabin specters. They'd stay about a week till they got ten or fifteen bucks, and they were going. Yeah. I guess some of them were that way. Some of them had a bedroll and then a gunny sack with a frying pan and a couple coffee cans or other things to cook a little suck in. And different lots of them, you know, never rode no place. They just keep hiking. Up there at St. Mary's branch, you know, they had a lot of people had breakfast in one camp, lunch at another one, and suffered another one. A lot of them, you know, only four or five miles apart. Some of them ten or fifteen. There was a lot of camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many guys that did that, just went through, didn't stay in work? Was that just a rare guy that did do that, or did a lot of guys camp to camp?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Probably the majority of them. We called them camp inspectors. They'd come along, stay a camp a couple days. We'd go to another one, and then they'd go out until they'd get the job they wanted someplace. I was keeping time up there one time.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: There was a fellow who used to make his rounds about every three or four years, and they'd hear him tell her he'd been all over the United States in the meantime, and maybe he was, nobody knew. But he was a good bullshitter. He came in one morning and he'd come to camp, and he had supper and stayed all night and breakfast, and he came in just to boss. Well, he said, I had supper last night, slept all night, breakfast this morning, the truck's pretty good, good place to sleep, I had a chance to go to work. The boss said, I don't know whether I can find anything for you or not. And now he said, don't over exert yourself, it won't take very much to keep me busy.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And I had car cancer, and they had a YMCA car, had a pool table and a phonograph and a preacher run the thing, he was getting fire and brimstone every Sunday. So he said, well, come on, I'll find something for you. So he took him to Saw Shack and got a saw, and the camp had just moved in there, hadn't been in there in a week or so. Log scattered all over the hill. There were a lot of buckskin drive stuff laying around there. He said, you can cut wood for that YMCA car.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: So he came in that night and he said, well, he said, you know what that fellow told me over there at that YMCA car? I said, no, what did he say?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He told me that me cutting wood for that car was the greatest act that had been done towards Christianity in a long, long time. He said, my credit ought to be good for a package of frillers, and sure is, I give him a package of frillers. He stayed about a week or so.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he came in one morning and he said, you're the official timekeeper here?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. Well, he said, I'd like that legal bill of separation between me and this corporation and the coastal newspaper. They call it a time check.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I give him his time check and he was going, oh, he had a wonderful line of baloney. He could just talk, but after a while, you know, it was too damn much. You couldn't go anymore. Well, I'll see you next year, maybe a year after. And away he goes. He never rode a train. Ollie had a box car. He didn't pay any fare. He'd hike in on the box car and go as far as that went.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: We had a funny thing. I was scaling for the Red Leads up at Clarky. I was scaling for the Red Leads up at Clarky, Idaho. So Fred and Charlie Warehouse would come in there. And they wanted to go up to Camp 3. So that was up the head of the other side of the incline. So I said, well, let's catch the log train up to the incline and we can walk from then into Camp 3. And they said, well, that's a good idea. So we got on the train, went up there, and then there was about 20 other guys on the train, too, going up to your job. So we walked from then in. In Camp 3, we can get in there at about 7 o 'clock in the evening. They didn't walk very fast, taking a life, taking a slow. They had lots of time. We went into the office there. They said, one of the fellows went in. They said, I'm sorry, we haven't got nothing for you. So we had no beds. We had no cooks or done for the day. And he said, we can't do a thing for you. So Charlie and Fred walked in. I was right behind them. And they said, we'd like to have accommodation for the night. We'd like to have something deep first. And I'd like to have something free for all these men out here. Well, he said, I'm very sorry, but we can't do nothing for you.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He says, you mean you couldn't do nothing for any man after they walk in here 10 miles to get to this camp and then you hadn't got a job or you can't give him nothing to eat or you won't let him sleep?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He says, I'm sorry. Well, now he says, I'll tell you. My name is Fred Weyerhaeuser and this is Charlie Weyerhaeuser. We own this, goddamn outfit. And he says, we want you to get those cooks out and roast them out and those flunkies and we want something to eat and we want it in the next hour. And he says, I want you to get some bedding and get some beds for these fellows and bed them down and also beds for us. My wife, we two of them, Joe, here. He's right along with us, three of us here. We'll sleep here in the office.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And now he let me tell you something, young boy. He says, along you work for the warehouse.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Don't you ever turn a man away from this camp hungry or without a bed?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: You can't find any place else you can make him a bed in the barn, get him some blankets, get him something for him, do something for him. And if we ever hear you ever turn in another man down for something to eat and a place to speak, you're job is done for us. Now don't forget that as long as you work for us. That's all I never did from then on. When anybody come along, he took care of us. Chris, I was getting a big cheek out of that. But they were a couple of fine old guys that way, you know. They just, they said, we've been all through the country and we never turned anybody down and we knew anything about it. He said, we're a big outfit and we can damn easy, we're well -affordin' to pay for these fellowships.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, when I was clerkin' up there, OTP, men were scarce, young boys all over the war. And he told me, he said, and then, well, if some guy quits, and he's a pretty good worker, he said, slip in my buck or two and make the check a dollar or so more than I'd come under. Give him a pair of socks or a patch in the back or something. Don't say nothin' about it to him, just don't charge him for it. And he'll think that he got the best of ya. And if he doesn't think that, he'll say, well, after he goes down and gets smoked on the way back, well, they treat me pretty good, I guess, for the backup.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And he'll, he's gotta have cash on the line to get anything known.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, God, I'll tell ya.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you used to pad it for him a little bit, huh?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You used to pat it a little bit for him. Give him a little bit extra.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Sure. Well, the footballers wouldn't have to work a day, a week, three, four hours or something like that. I bet in a lot of months up there that I gave way more stuff than one of those clerks would sell them today.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And men were hard to get in those days. It was a different thing, you know, than when you got a lot of men and so forth. And when you're up in the woods and you can't get men to do the work, you're up against it. So you gotta be a little free. That's why they leaned on you guys to get there.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That's your angle. There was a woman up there named Miss Pelton, state of manner. In this flu country, that was all horses. In those days, there was no machines, and every rancher had 10 or 15 teams of horses or 20, more than that, I guess, sometimes. One guy come up there and wanted a job -driving team.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: A woman looked at him and he said, where did you ever drive to?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, 20 heads on the Palouse, on the combine. He said, I can't use you. You haven't got enough horses.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Some of those, Bob, some of those guys stayed with him for 15 or 20 years. He stayed in the year or two. He'd say, I knew he wouldn't stay when he come here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this is Homer Pelton's father, huh? That Homer Pelton's father?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, it's Homer in Moscow, yeah.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Potlatch. Lives in Potlatch.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Is Ray in Pullman yet or do you know?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think he is.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, that was their dad.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: See, what were those three boys up in Bovill?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: One of them was an engineer and he went down, he went from there down on the Clearwater and was an engineer down there. And then one was a top loader and the other one, you know.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: It wasn't David, sir. David, David, David, a David boy.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: What ever happened to all of them?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: All of them.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: All of them? All of them?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: All but Henry.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Henry, where is he?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He's in a nursing home in Norcen. Oh, Lord. Not in a nursing home, he's in a private home. He was in a private. He and one sister, a nun that's in the hospital, Norcen, that's the only two that slept with him.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: What do you know?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this, Pelton's father, he was a foreman even before they came. He was a state of Maineer.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He logged back there and then he came to Minnesota and then to Minnesota out here. That's what a lot of them done. A lot of state of Maineers.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I was on my first drive, log drive, on the Susquehanna River in 1910.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That old river was a mile wide in lots of places. Rough and ready, I'll tell ya. You get on there with a drive, maybe 50 million feet of logs, a couple hundred drivers. In those days we went down an arks. We had arks and maybe 40 men do an ark. We put nowhere in the front and nowhere in the back. Put the horses on arks and take them right along with you. Strike a bar someplace and then you'd have to stop and get them out, get the horses to hold the logs off the bar into the river. See the river when it's big like that, it's always higher in the middle than it is the side and the logs keep going to the sides all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's where you get your start in logging?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That's right.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: There used to be an old Skinner, he's dead now a long time, but he ended up here in Spokane and got on relief or whatever you call it. He stayed sober for a year, he never drank anymore. But anyway, he was walking down the street one day down there and a guy vamed 15 cents.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: What do you want 15 cents for?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I said I want to catch the bus out on East Bay. Yeah, it's long as I like you. Walk, he said. I walk standpoint lots of times. And there's a lot of those guys. They didn't make it in one day, probably take them three or four days, but they'd jungle up on the highway.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Walking daily, he never rode anything. He walked all the way.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He did, huh?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I never heard of him. A guy named Walking Daily.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Daily, Walking Daily. He was a lumberjack.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He never stayed very long, and he played.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Did you ever get lined up on Dick Farrell?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He was the preacher, the lumberjack preacher. I've heard of him.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: What do you know about him? He's dead, doesn't he?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, he died a couple of years ago. Dick made all the camps around here, and he'd pre -commit him to camp. We always kept him in the office, and he'd head in the office for him. He'd go out and preach to the boys at night for about an hour. Then he'd go out and stay there, and the next day he'd go on to another camp, taking all the camps all over the country. One night there in camp, some young fella came in, a strapping young fella, you know. He started to make fun of Dick. Dick said, young man, there's nothing to make fun about.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Do you remember that?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he had a son that was a cook. The son was a flunking, and the old man was a flunking. The old man got cooking for small crews. Sam Quittleman used to be around the Black Hole Fernwood lot. Marsh. Quittleman, Sam Quittleman. And then he had a son, I forget now what the boy's name was. But anyway, he'd been to Potlatch there for a bowl of over. Flunking in there for quite a while, and he left and was gone two or three years. When he came back, they'd get him a job cooking at Camp 8. There was only about a dozen of us there. For dinner and supper, there'd be 25 or 30 there, maybe for breakfast. So he rang the doorbell, the gong dinner bell, one known. He came out and shook hands with all of us, and he knew us all. Of course, he knew him. He had Mickey Flynn. Well, Mickey, I learned a lot about cooking since I was here before. Yeah, yeah, Sam, but a lot of room for a chef.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But a lot of his what?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: A lot of room for improvement, yeah.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, this little fellow is the Lumberjack preacher. And so he stopped this fellow, he said, now hear him. Either you or I are going to go all walk out of here. Now, he said, I never had anybody interrupt my hand preaching when I'm preaching. That's preaching for God. He says, I'm here with preaching to try to make people see things better and live better. And it would teach you to listen to what I'm saying may help you a lot. And this fellow kept talking. Well, he says, I guess the only thing I can do is show you all. So he's called up, he goes down and tackles the guy. And the first thing we know here was the guy going right out the door, flying. Dick was a scrapper. He used to be a prize fighter back in Chicago, see. Now, boys, these fellas, he wasn't a big guy, you know. He's well proportioned for about five years. And by the way, he could fight, I'll tell you. He really could fight. He didn't take him long to get that fellow on the floor and drag him out that door. He was gone. He never came back in either. That's the only fight I ever heard of him having. And he came in there for 20 years.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: There was a cook up there. His name was Baker, a great big fat fellow. And all the way to the left, Chuck went to the left.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He was a good cook and a good fellow and everybody liked him. And anyway, before World War I, before we got into it and afterwards, you know, the camps were full of stock salesmen, mining stock, principally mining stock. And they all had a prospect hold on places, had a millionaire. And one salesman up there one time thought an old baker about his mine. Baker was interested in talking, asking questions about it. He just took them along, you know, to think that he was going to pump them, you know, and he'd get all the dope you go to out of them. And finally he said, well, I'll tell you. I said, I'm just working for 11, but I haven't got any money. But he said, I got a little invention here.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, how about trading some stock in my company to yours?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He had a piece of tin made like a figure eight, you know, with a hole in the top. And the guy looked at it and Baker kept the bullshit and what a good thing it was and everything.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: How was it going to be worth millions?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Kept reading them all.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And finally the fellow came out and asked him, well, what is that for?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he said, just to hang on the dog's tail, to keep the sun from trying to zap.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: So the salesman laughed.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I was clerking up there for Big Gil one time way up on Cougar Middle. Baker was cooking there too. And he was always jobbing somebody. Old Ed Connors was running a donkey there and his chippled john was tending hook for him. Anyway, he Connors had a shack. His wife was up there and she came to Spokane, was born for a week or two. And he started, got some chickens. And he was talking about the chickens and bullets. As soon as they started laying eggs, he said, I'll bring them over here. And he said to Baker, will you cook them for I .M.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Murphy and Big Gil?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: But he said, I won't eat a cook them. He was telling about, he read in the poultry journal, you can get chickens warm water and make them lay. And Baker said, well, he said, I won't eat a cook them and they'll all be hard boiled.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: What do you mean hard boiled? Why?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, you're giving them warm water. He said, they'll lay hard boiled eggs.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Why are you damn fooling a crazy kid?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he said, go ahead. So every day when Baker had slipped over there long in the afternoon and whatever fresh eggs was there, one or two, he'd put in hard boiled eggs. So it fell about over in the bottom of the cell one night.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And Baker said, what do you want them? Deviled or what?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I don't know what all. Well, he said, I want them fried.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, how are you going to fry a hard boiled egg?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And he cracked two or four. I told you, you can give an old damn chicken that looks warm water. They'd order warm boiled eggs. So the guy didn't say nothing. He said, I'll get even with him. So his wife wasn't back yet. So the guy killed a chicken and smeared blood all over his nightgown in the piss pots and everything was all blood. He got the other fella and he said, no. So over and tell Baker that I'm having hemorrhages and that you're going to town for the doctor and ask him if he'd come over and stay with me and you come back. Sure. So Baker come over and the guy was crawling up the wall and hooffin' and hollering blood all over. Baker get up quieted down and sitting in the rocking chair and pretty soon he'd doze off. It's about time he'd get off the guy and have another sip. Yep. Holler. That went on all night long.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: About four o 'clock in the morning the guy's asked Baker, he says, what time is it?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: They corrupted the clock. Four thirty. All right you son of a bitch, go home and get breakfast with the crew. I'll learn you to hard boil my eggs.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, he was quite a guy there old Baker.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: They had lots of good tricks in camp. That's what kept them human, you know, playing tricks on each other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tricks on each other?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That's nothing to do, you know, in the evenings and do something like that. You're not making music for the whole crew. And they put some really good story tellers. Boy, I tell you, they really put them out. Manufacture them. Think about anything, you know, and start a story on it. He's shooting a breeze on it. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: You know, he was about ten miles from Bogel, you know.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: This fella, when was the time that doctor was going to get here and Baker had calmed down?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, you know, it's ten miles. The guy had to go down there and back. Hell, he had to go, that's going to take two to three hours.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He went down there and back in fifteen minutes and they'd chew the rag and finally the guy can't pretend he'd open his eyes and wake up and, is that doctor here?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He says, no, no, just calm down and sleep. He'd be here and he went that way all night long. Until he's four o 'clock in the morning and he told him, go home and get back to the server.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the way that Dick Farrell used to preach, how he used to preach to the men? I mean, did he preach hard? Did he preach, did he threaten them or how did he do it?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: What kind of preacher he was. Just like a preacher, get in and give a sermon. Just like that, you know. I mean, you wouldn't say to him, you fellas are damned because you're not leading a good life. No, never let him to believe that they just told them to him on ice, just like any preacher would get up from the altar and give that sermon. You have to say anything. Give a sermon and all these are the good things and everything about the bad. You got to in any crack of joke or two, some kind, get him a laugh on your own. He was a typical showman, you know. He could go along and he'd get him very serious and then all at once he'd crack a joke and bring him all laughing, you know. And he'd get launched into it seriously again and he talked for about an hour. Very good. Most of the boys, we all liked him. Maybe we had just listened to him and tickled it in. But tickless commander, he's a regular rough and tumble guy. And we talked to him about prize fights and baseball games and everything like that afterwards. And giving the news from the outside, you know, the big camps doing this and the other camps doing that. And they all liked to hear him come. Every one of them liked him very much. He lived in Spokane, had a wife and two or four children. And he passed away just about a year ago.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: So if a guy came in the camp, there were other guys who would come in and everybody would listen to him. I mean, they'd like to hear him. They'd like to hear people with things about him. Back in the early days, the sisters up in Secret Heart used to come in the camp. They'd collect a dollar a month. That's Secret Heart Hospital up there, when that story started. They'd come in and collect a dollar a month from everybody with men there. And they'd stay overnight. We all, we never had no place where I'm saying we'd have to go out in the bunkhouse and leave them to have the office. And they'd come in every month regular, collect the money and go around all the camps there. Of course, we'd have them 200 men, they'd leave there the five or six hundred dollars every time. And they'd come in and that's the way the kids would take it hard off. There were art workers and those men respected them, you know, whether they were Catholics or Protestant or anything else. They didn't make any difference. I heard that some of those cooks, that some of the cooks were pretty temperamental. Some of the cooks were good, some of them were bad, and it was all...

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, a good cook and a good blacksmith was Crabier and Hill. They were all damn hard to get along with. Yes, if they're good, they're always cranky. I know I was in the blister rust one time up here, the way up above Nordman, above Fries Lake. He had a hell of a good cook. Yeah, he was a good cook, but he was broke. He thought he was quite a bullshitter. He thought he was going to get kind of a superintendent cook or something to look after all the camps. Well, he didn't plan anything like that. He landed just camp. I was in the canyon, Hill went straight up on his side. I just laughed. Now gone. He wasn't supposed to put a stovepipe through the tent. You were supposed to run it out and all the damn stoves wouldn't draw. He'd stand there with a pipe in, you know, fan on the stove and turn in the hotcakes and the other one cutting the broomstick. Finally, I had been down to one of the other camps and went in and had lunch with the guy there. The cook said, I was telling him I was having trouble with the stove. Well, he said, we did too, but we shot that pipe straight up and cut this. He said, I haven't had any trouble since. It's worth trying. But he said, it won't work the other way out. No, it won't end up outside. So I went home and back to camp. The next day I said, I'm going to leave a couple of fellas in here and I think we're going to fix that stove. Oh, the damn thing is no good. There never was any good. Well, I said, there's hundreds of them here in this. Every damn camp the Forest Service has got here has got a stove like that. It must be the exception if you're the only one who's no good. Well, all right, go ahead and fix it. So we shot the pipe up through that cut this seam.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Once a month, the checks weren't coming. We had about two months, over two months coming. And I went down. There was a cook down there at the surfer where the upper lake and lower priest come together. There was a cook down there. There was a barber and he was cutting hair. So I got my haircut and I come up and I didn't see him that night. The next morning, I did a breakfast. He asked, hey, come here.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: What do you want? What in the hell did you get that haircut?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, down here at the surfer, down here at the camp, whatever it was. God, he said, I need a haircut.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: How in hell am I going to get a haircut?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I've been here a damn year, two months and haven't got a paycheck yet. I said, come on, I'll pay for your haircut. We'll go down tonight. And it was five or six miles walking down. So we went down and it was right after payday and that surfer, there was a bootleg in there. I forget now what the hell that guy was doing. Anyway, I had bought him a couple drinks and his haircut. I'd only run on his mother cook that he knew. The other cook had got paid and so he helped blow him in a little bit anyway. About midnight, we started back to camp. I don't got any of them making. Anyway, we tried. He had a tomato can, those gallon cans, you know, and stuck a hole in the bottom and put a candle in there and a piece of haywire on for a letter. It works pretty good. Anyway, we left the surfer. That meadow there was just like walking on carpet, spongy, or water under eight. He got to the edge of the meadow and there was a crick. Well, not a crick, but more than one of the cricks. But he went over in the ditch maybe a couple feet away and he had a temple cross set. Well, he didn't make it and he landed in there and kind of quicksand. And then he started going down all our bloody murder. Two or three guys come running over there and we pulled them out. And we started up the trail. Every once in a while, let's sit down a while now. I want to sleep. I'll do hell with your sleep. I'll finally let him sleep a few minutes and then give him a hell of a good kick in the sole of his shoe, you know. He'd wake up and cuss me and he'd want to kill me and every other damn thing. We'd go on for a mile or two further and he'd want to lay down again. So I let him lay down for ten or a human incident, give him another kick in the sole of the shoe. I got to camp and there was a pretty good size crick there. And it was about half as wide as this building. There was a log across there, a foot. Horses and mules, water courses and waited across the creek. I'd say he used to walk and then used to go across there. And he was still drunk and he almost had been wandering between one and two in the morning.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And how in hell am I going across there?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I said, get on your ass and work your way across. Yeah, walk across and do it that way. Well, anyway, about a lot in the middle and he fell in. Cuss and sore and everything. And I thought, well, there won't be any breakfast this morning. Somebody will have to get in. And that guy, he was up and at it. And Brecker did the same as. Nothing happened. So with that drunk done him good, he kind of quieted him down and about the next night checked.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, a good cook, you had to let him go about every three months. You didn't let him go to town every three months for at least four or five days a week. But he assumed, you know, he'd be hitting the extract bottle. He could go three months. And then I used to take him and they put three months in. I'd give him a week in town. And if they came back and be there the next Monday morning, they'd pay go on. And if they wouldn't, that'd be it. He'd have it. I'd have another cook. So it worked out pretty good that way because they'd get out of town and blow in and get drunk and then come back and go to work again. Be good for another three months. But they cooked still, but they had a lot of good cooks in this country, best in the country. Now, you had Benny Marsh up there at Fernwood. He ran the hotel there at Fernwood. He used to cook on all these camps. And then he went down to Clearwater and he cooked there for years. And his son is still cooking there. And that, from the day they started, that's back about 1922, around there.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: There was Marsh and Marsh. There was Billy Marsh in the pot lights. Yeah, that's right. He was a hell of a good cook, too. Oh, crack attack. So he got doggy. Chuck was kind of scarce and far between anyway. A fellow saying, telling him about it, he said, you know, this colored candy they put on cakes. Christmas time, a little bit of fine stuff, pink and red and blue. I said, that son of a bitch has got it on the hotcakes now. No, he's got it on the donuts now. He said, the next thing you'll have it on the hotcakes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard they did gyppo cooking. They went to that. Some guys worked out good as gyppo cooks and some guys didn't work out good on that.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, gyppo cooks never done much good, unless he was a good cook. If he was a good cook, well, you got along fine. But the most of them wasn't much for it. They started skimping on the food and then the men started kicking. And it didn't work out.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, as they started that, you know, when they started gyppo and logging, they started gyppo and the cookout. And that didn't work out so good. Mike Joe said if he got a good cook, it was all right. If he wouldn't stay, he wouldn't know.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: The board was 60 cents a day and then 75 and then 90 and a dollar and a dollar and a quarter. I think it was a dollar and a quarter when I left. Now, I guess it's two or three dollars a day. If there's any camps or any camps anywhere, speak up.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: If you want men to work, you've got to feed them. If you don't feed them, you're not going to have any good workers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that Chuck was good in most of the camps? Do you think that Chuck was good in most of the camps?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, better than just as good as he gets at that board. Better. Better than you're getting at that board hotel.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: More of it.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And those lovely Jacks worked hard and more they could eat food, I tell you.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Three or four kinds of vegetables, three or four kinds of meat, three or four kinds of pastries, a couple kinds of pie. Tell the table they have double deckers, they had a shelf up here with all the pastry on. There wasn't room on the table.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Some of those fellas would eat 15, 20 hotcakes in the morning for breakfast. Plus maybe five or six eggs and a pound or two of bacon. Yeah, boy, I could really put it away. In the evening I'd often see fellas put two T -bone steaks on his plate. Plus everything else along with it.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: My name's Big Gil. He was down here one time in the old Rockaway and he ordered a dozen eggs and two T -bone steaks. And the hasher thought he must have been off the rocker or something. She brought him one steak and three or four eggs. He looked up at her, that kind of thing, and he said that's a hell of a meal to put down in front of him. I'll order two steaks and a dozen eggs and that's what I want. She forgot to rest.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: You know when he died, when Big Gil...

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Big Gil Pippen?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. He had the funeral service up there. The Hennepin's had his square old.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: On third there was that plumbing outfit, isn't it?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. I went to the Rosary that night, 10 close there. And this Big Gil sister was married to another Frenchman that had a hotel in Sloane and Iowan. He was a little short fellow, not any taller than I am, but Lord, he had a belly on him. So, it was as big as his table. And he was standing there, Joe... Dan Close was talking to him and the three sons were over on the other side of the funeral parlor. One of them is dressed up here and they just chopped out a heart paper and marks window. The other fellow was a priest and then there was a rounder. He was clean, but he just had another work. Dan Close was a little... And those three -year sons over there, Mr. LeGaux. Yes. Yeah, that French... I had the same fellow say back east, I didn't do so bad. He said, I got one priest, one dude, and one son of a bitch.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: His wife, LeGaux's wife came down here. At that time, the pedicord, everybody stayed at the pedicord, all the lumberjacks and the Canadian line, I guess, to... Southern Idaho and Oregon. But anyway, this son of a bitch, he was always drunk. He never worked. Just bummed and bummed and begged. Anyway, he knew his mother was in town and found that out some way, so he went up to the hotel and stole her purse. So she went out someplace, when she came back, her horse was gone, so she went to the clerk and she said, I'm going to somebody's store my purse. You call that papa, and I owe him, tell him I want some money. So the clerk called that papa and she got on the bone. Papa, I lost my money, I lost my purse. Somebody stole my purse. I ain't got no money, send me some money. You didn't lose your purse, Mama.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: You see Freddie?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: No, when Freddie got your purse. The old man knew it. Yeah. Freddie. He used to come up there about, oh, maybe once or twice a year or two or three or four months. He'd come up there and he'd be shaking, you know. Big gill, I'd put him to work. In fact, he'd do nothing for two or three days to get over the shakes and sober it up, and then he'd give him his job. He'd work two or three months, and then away he'd go again. Then the mother would get, get big gill to get it up there and sober him up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many lumberjacks that didn't drink?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, maybe one out of a hundred. About one out of a hundred that didn't drink. Lumberjack. There wasn't very many.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: No. Well, everybody drank.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Hard drinking, hard workers.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Hard worker, cranky in hell. I was just a punk, 21 years old when I was up there. And the crew was, the majority was middle aged or older. There was very few.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And some of those old fellows would say, good morning.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: What the hell is good about it? How do you like the weather today? What do you care about it?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Baliel. Oh, you couldn't even look at me in Baliel. But there was one old fellow up there, old Tom Bowman, who was all crippled up with rheumatism, walked with his scale. He was always happy. Ha, ha, ha, ha. I thought, I hope I go through life with that little distribution. And I have up till now, I don't know. He was always laughing, always happy. And you knew damn well he was in pain a lot of the time. He was never crabby. But nine out of ten of those older fellows, you know, gosh, and Baliel, if you look at them. When you say this fellow used to be working in the woods until 75, 80 years old, you know, they just tough it in the whale world.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Old George Foss in one of the old men up there, a log roller. And George lived, he's 82. And he could get right out of the log in the middle of the team and burn handsprings on it. I see him do that when he was 80. Just, he's done all his life.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yep. That's Clay Hall Brown. He was another one. He was 80 somewhere. The driving team, it was called Clay Hall Brown. I see him coming down the road. The trail was a log, a log roller on one of my, it's on top of the other side, you know, on the log. Sure.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: But you take those fellows, you know, they went, they worked. They get out, after a couple days work, you get that liquor all out of them, sweat like a soda, just take a son of that, you know. And take about two days and they were cleaned up. Then they'd go to bed at 9 o 'clock at night and get up at 5 in the morning. That's what I did, you know, eight hours in. And they'd, very industrious, they'd come in and spend their money like a drunken sailor. They'd put it up in camp. They'd sew up their own clothes. They'd wash their own clothes. And so was anything to sew or anything like that. And they'd all be smoking a coin cup pipe. And not have them roll on their own and smoke cigarettes. They'd always roll their own. Everybody had them already made. But they lived a good life. And they'd stay sober when they was working in logging camps, when they was out logging. They'd stay sober as long as they couldn't get any liquor. Of course, if anybody brought any liquor in, they always helped them drink it. Well, my guess is wasn't there a lot of moonshining going on in those days with the stuff coming into the... But we never had much trouble with it in camp. Sometimes somebody'd get in once in a while, but as a rule, you didn't love drinking in camp and that was it. If somebody bought a bag, bottled in at his back sack or something like that, they'd all help him drink it. But with a quarter whiskey wouldn't last only about three minutes among a bunch of them, Jacks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So would you say that too, Dan, that there wasn't much drinking on a job, wasn't much drinking in the camps?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, they might be. Get a little too much some night or somebody brought in somewhere. But the next morning they always went to work. They didn't bother me anyway. They were laying around drunk for a week or anything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the guys have to wait until Spokane to get their women?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, some of these small towns, most of them, St. Mary's. St. Mary's only.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: St.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Mary's was wet and then St.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Mary's wasn't dry. Then they had to go to Montana to get it.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Now I was just thinking about the women, about what they did for that. Because I know that they did up in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what they did mostly? They waited until they had a chance to blow into Spokane like you were saying?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That's right. St. Mary's, well, Lewiston always had lots of girls. When the Clearwater started down there, and then St. Mary's had a lot from up in there. St. Joe and on to St. Mary's. A lot of girls lived down the riverboats down there right in town. And then the rest of them came into Spokane pretty much.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: It was an old fellow there at Camp 8, an old Frenchman, and he had a white, black and white, black spots and white, hired your skin order to call it black and white, I don't know what it means. I don't know what it would all do.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He had about a dozen houses built in.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Anyway, he used to work for a while, a week or two, when he'd get 10 to 15 bucks, go to St. Mary's, and hurt a clerk and walk over and walk back or come back on the train. And a carrying bedroll, and then he'd get three or four bottles of liquor and moonshine and whatever he had. And then when Milwaukee used to come to Camp 8, they used to stop there, and they'd get off the train and everybody'd see him coming in new way. So I was the first one up to the bunkhouse to go over there. And he and the dog was in the bunk, the bedroll, and I thought, you know, you're fooling with that bedroll and that damn dog, he chapped. So I went back down to the shop. I said to somebody, Billy Watts, he's in the bed sleeping, but I said, the damn dog is in there with him on, and you're going to get to bed. Come on, we'll get it. So we went up to the, we went in the bunkhouse and walked around and looked everything over, and everything up, and Bill says, oh, leave that door open and you stand in that door, and we'll get that damn dog out of here. Oh, he went again out of here. He said, I'll get him out of here. And up here at the bunkhouse, he stopped to stand for sweet and easy, and the dog raised up and never heard on his back standing up, and walked to the back up a few of the steps he would have given the dogs to keep from the head. And back and up, and finally get outside and the dog out and up the door, and he knows if he ever kicked anything in the ass, he'd take that dog, in the court shoes, and I just ripped his old mind and everything around. And so Bill came in and cut the door, and I cut the bed door. I'll see you at the court.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I guess that about winds it up.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Anything more?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah, there's some more I'd like to ask you, just because this is the only chance I'll have to talk to you about. So I'm enjoying it a lot, the stories. If you want to, we could break for a bit and then talk some more, if you'd rather. No, I think we better get through. I'm not run down. I'm not run down. Let me ask you then, we'll just talk for... Yeah, but I got an appointment at four o 'clock, so... Oh, well let me ask you about it. Okay, let me ask you then, there's one more thing I'd like to ask you about. That's the IWW, about what you know about the old, you know, the disturbances, the strikes in the woods. Well, the strikes, I was running camp at that time with the Panhandling, we come near Spirit Lake, and we used to pull the strike every Friday night. And it all come, I'd stay up all night, and I'd help the bookkeeper write the checks and pay him all off after breakfast in the morning. They'd come in town, and then I'd come in town Sunday, and I'd hire them all back again and take them back on the train Monday morning. And one summer, they went out 14 times during the summer. When they come to September, I said, I got the boy, and I said, well, things are getting pretty bad.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, what the hell are you folks going to do next winter?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: You're not going to have any money. You're spending it every week this year, you get the paycheck every Friday night, and you both have no winter, and you can put in the winter. Well, they said, we're getting damn tired of going out. So he says, if you... Now this was the second year of mobbing trouble, IWW, and he said, I got this. Come in, pull the camp, and away they go. And he says, if you'll back us up, if you'll drive those fellows out of camp when they come in tonight, he says, I got, we'll be back out of here. So they come in, and about a couple of boys come in the office and say, Mr. Maloney said, two boys out there, they're going to pull the camp tomorrow morning. I said, okay, thanks. So I put on a new pair of court shoes and walked out.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Now, you gentlemen get a ticket for a job here?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And he said, no, we haven't. I said, well, I'm sorry, we have nothing for you. We're filled up. We don't have no bedroom. We haven't got anything else. And if you had a ticket, you'd even have to sleep in the bar. But I said, you fellows are not...

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Are you looking for work?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: No, we didn't come in to work. He said, we're going to pull the camp in the morning. Well, I said, you're not going to pull this camp in the morning. I said, you're all through. I said, now you get the hell out of here, and I'll give you three minutes to hit that door.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: They looked at one another, and he sat there two minutes, and I said, well, boy, he's got one minute. They grabbed his stuff and away they went. And that was the last walkout we had. And they walked ten miles down the scared lake, stayed there that night. And the next morning the paper come out, Maloney, the panhandle gunman, and he had a nice crew at the point of two guns last night, forced the Wobbly organizers out of camp, and they had to walk ten miles to get to a place so they could find a bed for the night.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Now, that's just one of the things, like the Wobbly, you know. And they'd come in and they... Well, they'd come in the camp, they'd cut somebody's cork shoes all the hell because you wouldn't join them, cut his clothes out, stuff like that, you know.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, then that'd make the rest of the boys mad, see?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And so they wouldn't last long. You'd drive them out. But they called the hell of a lot of trouble. Of course, they'd done a good job when you're going to look back on it. They'd done a damn good job because the conditions in camps before the Wobbly's was rotten. You had carried their own bedding. Some guy came in, he'd be lousy. He'd louse up the whole camp. And stuff like that, you know. And they'd steal. They'd come in and steal the fellas. They'd steal their cork shoes. If they had anything good, jackets and stuff like that. And of course, that made the band mad. And of course, you had a fight, you had the 4Ls, and you had the IWWs. And they were just back and forth in the quarter.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Of course, most of them joined both of them, see? Oh, they did?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: So, yeah. So they belonged to both of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't the IWW headquarters in Spokane here?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Down at Bernardin, see Bernardin, Maine. They had a place down there, and they had a bookkeeping, in fact, what broke them up. They'd done something, a fellow would die out in the woods. And then the secretary down here in Spokane would send out to all the woodsmen and find, send in a dollar to help bury him. Well, hell, every month, maybe a couple of guys died, and they'd be chomping in.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And they got a lot of money that way, see?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And finally, the end of it was, the top men were getting ready to pull out. And the secretary, he wasn't in on it. So by God, one night, and he had to charge his money, he had $50 ,000 in the bank, and he pulled the $50 ,000 in the bank and left town. Nobody ever heard of him. Now, that busted up the IWWs right there. Of course, it was getting pretty well. They'd got all the demands, they fixed up the camps. When they went now, they went out on strike all over the country, and they had the jungle camps. They put them in, you know. And they had a place up at Spirit Lake, they had 200 out there. One night, I got a call up in the woods from the general manager, Mr. Higginboe. He said, I'd like to have you come down, Joe. I want to talk to you about some serious talk tomorrow. And I said, okay. So I went down that Saturday night, and we had a meeting the next day.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And he says, how's things going? Do you think you can get started up soon?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, sure, but just in the spring, see, about 50th of March, 1st of April. And he said, well, he said, he's been feeding these guys here, he says, now for a couple months. And he says, the city is almost broke, and they can't continue with any law. Now, if you can start up, if you're ready to start up, maybe we can take these men off with their hands. I said, well, sure, I'd be glad to. So he says, if you stay over tonight and not get back till tomorrow, and he says you can go up in the boat, you can order the boats for tomorrow morning, take the whole crew out. And I said, well, if the city will let them go without any fines or anything like that, well, that'll be fine. So I went up and talked to the city council, and I said, well, gentlemen, I'd be glad to relieve you of all these fellas and take them up to camp and put them to work.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He said, you mean you'll take those guys up and work with them?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I said, sure, they're some of the best men in the country out there.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: So he says, all right, if you'll take those men out of here, he says, when do you want to do it?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, right now. Okay, he says, if they'll go, well, you can take them free of charge, no charges. We'd be very grateful to you for taking them. So I went out and got up on a bar, I talked to the boys, I'm opening up the camps, I'd be glad to give you fellas all jobs. But we won't need your blankets anymore, you can throw them away, and all you need is your clothes, your court shoes, whatever clothes you have. But we won't allow blankets in camps anymore. So we took the gang, they just towed away their blankets, and I marched them down to town, too oppressed, got them all on the boats, had two boats there, a big platform boat, you know, and took them all up to camp and started operations the next morning. And I never had any more IWW trouble after that. For about three years, they sure give them hell. But they got clean sheets, they got a single blimp, they used to come in the camp, and he'd come in the camp with a job, and you'd go out there, you'd have to give him a suit for somebody you didn't even know.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And you'd have your own blankets, you'd need to have his, see?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, they're all double beds, and I got them, after they went out on strike there, they were out on strike about, it was almost four months. In the meantime, we built a recreation place for them, we put in char baths, put in washing machines where you could wash their clothes, before that they went down the creek and built a little fire and washed their clothes, and put in washroom, all of them couldn't take a bath every night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean while they were on strike, you were fixing up the camps?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That's right, we were fixing up everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When year was that, you remember?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, that was about 19...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 1919, wasn't it?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: No, no, before that, about 1915. I joined the Army in 1919. Well, it wasn't over, though, it was over. I joined the Army in 1917, and it was before 1917, 1915 and 1916. Yeah, it was about 1917, I think.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: That was the last one, it run from 1915 and 1917.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the camps striking around Bovill?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Some of them, you see. Some of them were quit and some of them stayed, there was never no whole clue left.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: But... This all went on, what you're seeing up around Spirit Lake. Well, it was all over the North.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: They blowed that thing all out of the portion. Oh, yeah, sure.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: They would go out on strike, and I'd come back in town and hire the whole damn bunch back again, and be back. There were no trouble, the finest fellas in the world. These fellas came in, ordered them to strike, and all, and there wasn't much they could do about it. A few radicals, but the most of the guys didn't go very excited about it. A lot of guys, a lot of lumberjacks were in it, though. I know, I was... You had to join, and you couldn't live in the camp, you didn't have a card.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I was croaking up there, little mob, in about 40 quid and about 40 stays.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: So... They glued it all...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: stool pigeons ?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Yeah. Had an old fella from, I think his name was Jordan, he was deputy sheriff or something over there in the musk. They had those guys, and they caused more damn trouble than the Wobbly did. Sure. And anyway...

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: But the worst thing the Wobbly did was they'd pull a strike and all come to town.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Getting drunk and then come back. Anyway, this whole company was stupid, and he came in after... I was 40 of them quid anyway, I had paid out all their checks. He came in, put on these glasses, and got out his pencil, and he wanted a piece of paper. I gave him a piece of paper, and he wanted to write letters. He says, I want the names, and everybody quit here today.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I said, what the hell do you want?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I didn't want those young things, I treat me right. I said, no, you damn, I don't know who and I don't give a damn. I said, if you want the names, if everybody has quit here, go down to the office in Bobo or the auditor in Potlatch. They'll put out the information I ain't putting out any... I ain't not getting paid for putting on...

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: How long is my job going to last here?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: If every damn fool comes in here and wants to know everything about all the company's business, I'd be out here the second day. So, old Bob said, that's tell them, kid.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: He says, I am going to challenge something a mouthful some of these days.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: It was long, three or four days, and old TP came up. And this whole stool came in. In the meantime, oh, any guy come along in the world, old Bob says, no, give a damn what he can do, and I didn't take a seat at the table anyway, fill that up. And there was a goofy guy come up here, he put him to work. He worked four or five days a week. After he left, old TP came up, and there was one of inspection to earn. He and Bob was sitting in the office, and I was sitting in the stool, stool vision committee, and he said, Mr. Jones, your brother had a fellow here working about a week. He shouldn't have around here at all. He was absolutely crazy. Old Bob jumped up and he called him everything, and he didn't go and get your bed and get the hell out of here. I wanted to tell you what I thought about you for a long time. TP said, hold on, Bob, hold on, hold on by a damn sight. He's no good, never was any good. He's more trouble than all the wobbly's put together, and get them the hell out of here. TP took him down on the speeder. They transplanted him to camp down here, during the summer, and old Bob took him.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: Well, I remember right, they had a big, all the companies. There was Potlatch and Forest and the Diamond match and the Federal match and the Humber and the Panhandle. All these big companies were out here. They had a big meeting here, it was in August. And they all agreed to fix up the camps and put things in good condition. They had single beds, no double beds anymore, one over the other. All single beds, sheets and pillowcases. They raised the board to a dollar and five cents a day. They raised the wages to six dollars a day. And that was in the latter part of August, 1917. We then, after they made this, we were all into these meetings. We met here in Spokane for two weeks. And then when they agreed to do this, we went back and Panhandle at Spirit Lake on Monday morning. Old Manhagan Boo was the gentleman who then called us all in, told us to go back and operate and start the camps and get them all operating.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: So we did.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: And then on everything was fine, then we got our foothold after that. And then we started gyppoing after that. And the gyppoing, the first Wobblies, some of the worst Wobblies they had were the first ones to go gyppoing. So it just crucified our organization. I met that guy that left here, the Secretary, down in Los Angeles about two years after that, that took the fifty thousand dollars. Well, he said, Joe, he says, I seen what was happening and these boys were patting their pockets. And he said, by God, I better get the hell out of here before there's anything, won't be anything left. And he said, I had fifty thousand dollars up there. So he said, I went up and got it and I flew the coup. And they never heard, never caught up to him, never found him or anything. The organization busted up. Oh, I gotta get home. They kept an office down here in Bernard Street, had a little small office in there, and they kept that open for about three months and then they go.

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: All of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you're going home?

speaker: Joe Maloney or Unknown Speaker
words: I'm about to take you home. Yeah, that's good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's good. Thanks a lot for...
